title_en: Ibn Hajar al-Haytami: Life and Scholarly Formation
title_ar: ابن حجر الهيتمي: حياته وتكوينه العلمي
content_en:
Shihab ad-Din Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Ali ibn Hajar al-Haytami was born in 909 AH (1504 CE) in Abu Haytam, a village in the Nile Delta of Egypt. He studied in Cairo at al-Azhar under some of the most eminent scholars of his era, including Zakariyya al-Ansari, who was also the teacher of ar-Ramli — a circumstance that makes the later divergence between Ibn Hajar and ar-Ramli particularly interesting, since they were, in a sense, scholarly siblings from the same master. Ibn Hajar's education was deep and broad, encompassing the full range of Islamic sciences.

Around 948 AH (1541 CE), Ibn Hajar left Egypt and settled in Mecca, where he spent the remainder of his long life. He taught in the sacred mosque, wrote prolifically, and issued fatwas that were consulted across the Islamic world. His physical presence in the Hijaz, combined with the prestige of Mecca as the center of Muslim pilgrimage, gave his scholarly authority a unique reach: pilgrims who came from Indonesia, India, East Africa, and elsewhere would attend his lessons, carry his books home, and disseminate his legal positions across the Shafi'i world. This is the primary reason why his positions came to dominate the Shafi'i tradition in Southeast Asia and East Africa.

His scholarly output is vast. Beyond Tuhfat al-Muhtaj, he wrote al-Zawajir an Iqtiraf al-Kaba'ir (a treatise on major sins), Fath al-Jawwad (a commentary on Irshad al-Ghawi by Ibn al-Muqri), and numerous other works in jurisprudence, theology, and hadith. His fatwa collections preserve detailed responses to the most contested legal questions of his era. He died in Mecca in 974 AH (1567 CE), having shaped the Shafi'i tradition for generations. His long residence at the heart of the Islamic world, combined with his prolific output and the wide circulation of his works through pilgrimage networks, gave his scholarship a geographic reach that few scholars based in a single city have equaled. The result was a form of scholarly authority that transcended regional Shafi'i sub-traditions, making Tuhfat al-Muhtaj a reference consulted as readily in Hadhramaut and Sumatra as in Cairo and Damascus.
